title: Build Day 1
subtitle: From canvas to code — bringing your AI project to life

diagram.alt: The build loop: plan, prototype, test with a real user, and improve

overview: Today is pure building. You'll work on your chosen project track, with AI as your helper. The goal by end of day is a working prototype — even a rough one — that demonstrates your core idea.

Remember the Design Thinking principle: a bad prototype you can test beats a perfect plan you can only imagine. Done is better than perfect on Day 8. AI is your collaborator on this build, not a shortcut — you still make the decisions about what to build and why.
objectives: Have a working proof-of-concept by end of day
Identify the biggest technical or design challenge in your project
Test your prototype with at least one person outside your team
Document what works, what doesn't, and what you'll fix tomorrow

concepts.heading: Done Is Better Than Perfect
body: On a build day, your job is not to finish a polished product — it's to get something real that works enough to test. A rough prototype you can put in front of a person teaches you more than a beautiful plan that only lives in your head.
Pick the smallest version of your idea that still shows the core concept, build that first, then improve it. You can always add more once the heart of it works.

text: A bad prototype you can test beats a perfect plan you can only imagine.
AI is a collaborator, not a shortcut — you still own the ideas, the choices, and the testing.
Build the core idea first. Polish comes later.

activities.name: Track A — Smart Chatbot
intro: Tools: Claude.ai or ChatGPT (free tier) + Google Docs to record outputs.
A smart chatbot for a specific purpose is built through a carefully crafted system prompt — the instruction that defines who the AI is, what it knows, and how it should behave.
steps: Copy this system-prompt template and fill in the [brackets]: "You are [name], an AI assistant specialising in [topic]. You help [target user] with [specific task]."
Set the personality: "Your personality is [3 adjectives — e.g. warm, patient, encouraging]."
Write the rules it always follows: only answer questions related to [topic], and politely redirect off-topic ones.
Add a positive rule ("Always [key behaviour — e.g. show your working step by step]") and a prohibited rule ("Never [behaviour — e.g. give the answer without explanation]").
Add a safety rule: "If you are unsure, say so clearly and suggest where the user could find a reliable answer."
End every response with a follow-up question to check understanding.
Test it: paste your system prompt into Claude or ChatGPT as the first message, then have a classmate try to use your chatbot. Does it behave as intended?
facilitator: Help teams keep the system prompt focused — the most common mistake is trying to make one chatbot do everything.
Encourage teams to save each version of their prompt in Google Docs so they can compare what changed.

activities.name: Track B — Image Classifier App
intro: Tools: Teachable Machine (teachablemachine.withgoogle.com) + a simple index.html file.
Build path: train a model in Teachable Machine, export it, and embed it in a tiny web app.
steps: Phase 1 — Train your model (30 min): plan your 3–4 categories the classifier will recognise.
Collect training images: at least 100 per class, from multiple angles, lighting, and backgrounds.
Train on Teachable Machine and evaluate accuracy — aim for 85%+ before moving on.
Phase 2 — Export and embed (30 min): click "Export Model" → "Tensorflow.js" → "Upload my model" and copy the model URL.
Open a new file called index.html, paste in the Teachable Machine sample code from the "Code snippets" section, and replace the placeholder model URL with yours.
Open index.html in your browser — it should work immediately.
Phase 3 — Customise (30 min): change the page title, add a description, and show a friendly message when each class is detected.
Share your index.html file with another team and let them test it.
facilitator: Watch for low accuracy caused by too few or too-similar training images — more variety per class fixes most problems.
Keep a known-good copy of the Teachable Machine sample code handy for teams who break their index.html.

activities.name: Track C — AI Story Generator
intro: Tools: Claude.ai or ChatGPT + Google Slides or PowerPoint.
Concept: the player makes choices, and each choice is fed back into the AI prompt to generate the next scene — an interactive branching story.
steps: Phase 1 — Story architecture (20 min): choose a setting and premise, e.g. "You are a young scientist in 2080 who discovers an AI that has gained emotions."
Map your story tree: 3 chapters, each with 2 choices — that's 8 possible story paths.
Write the world-building context (2–3 paragraphs) the AI will use as background.

Phase 2 — System prompt design (20 min): set the tone, e.g. "You are a narrative AI for an interactive science fiction story. The tone is [adjective]. Always end each scene with exactly 2 choices formatted as: CHOICE A: [option] | CHOICE B: [option]."
Test the prompt: does the AI keep the story consistent? Does it remember previous choices?
Phase 3 — User interface (40 min): build a simple Google Slides or PowerPoint deck. Slide 1 is the opening scene with 2 buttons linking to Slide 2A or 2B.
For each branch slide, generate that scene from the AI using the chosen option as context.
Continue for 3 chapters — you'll end up with roughly 14 slides total.
facilitator: Remind teams that the AI doesn't remember between slides on its own — they must paste the prior choice back in as context.
Help teams scope down if 8 paths feels too big; even a 2-chapter, 4-path story demonstrates the concept.

activities.name: Track D — AI Research Assistant
intro: Tools: Claude.ai or ChatGPT + Google Docs.
Concept: design a series of prompts that help a student research any topic responsibly — a 5-prompt research system.
steps: SCOPE PROMPT: "Given the research question [X], break it into 5 specific sub-questions that together would fully answer it."
SOURCES PROMPT: "For the sub-question [Y], suggest 5 types of sources I should look for. Include at least 2 primary source types."
SUMMARISE PROMPT: "Here is text I found [paste source text]. Summarise the key points relevant to [sub-question]. Flag any claims that need verification."
BIAS CHECK PROMPT: "Analyse this summary for potential bias, missing perspectives, or unsupported claims: [paste summary]."
SYNTHESIS PROMPT: "Using these verified points [list], write a 3-paragraph evidence-based response to [original research question]. Cite each point."
Test your system: use it to research a topic you actually need for school. Does it produce better research than googling alone?
facilitator: Push teams to actually run the full chain on a real topic — the value only shows up when all 5 prompts work together.
Use the BIAS CHECK step as a teaching moment about verifying AI output rather than trusting it.

activities.name: Daily Build Log
intro: Complete this with your team at the end of Day 8. It becomes your starting point for tomorrow.
steps: What did you get working today?
What's the trickiest part right now?
What's your #1 goal for tomorrow?
One thing you're proud of:
One thing you'd do differently:
How's your teamwork going (if you're building with others)?
What do you want to figure out or get help with next time?
facilitator: Collect or photograph each team's build log — you'll use it to open the Day 9 stand-up.
Make sure every team has tested with at least one person outside their team before they fill this in.

reflection: What is the core idea your prototype needs to prove — and does it prove it yet?
Where did AI genuinely help you, and where did you have to make the real decisions yourself?
What surprised you when someone outside your team tried your prototype?
If you could only fix one thing tomorrow, what would it be and why?

vocab.definition: An early, rough version of a project built to test the core idea.
A working demo that shows your main idea actually works.
The instruction that tells an AI who it is, what it knows, and how to behave.
Improving something through repeated rounds of building and testing.

quiz.q: What is the main goal by the end of Build Day 1?
options: A working proof-of-concept — even a rough one — that demonstrates your core idea
A completely finished, bug-free product ready to sell
A written plan describing what you will build later
A slideshow with no working parts
quiz.explain: Day 8 is about getting a working prototype you can test. Done is better than perfect — a rough proof-of-concept beats a polished plan.

quiz.q: On a build day, AI is a shortcut that does the project for you so you don't have to make decisions.
quiz.explain: False. AI is a collaborator, not a shortcut. You still own the ideas, the choices, and the testing — the AI helps you build them.

quiz.q: How is a purpose-built chatbot (Track A) mainly created?
options: By writing a carefully crafted system prompt that defines who the AI is and how it behaves
By training a brand-new AI from scratch with millions of images
By buying a finished chatbot from a store
By drawing the chatbot's face in a slideshow
quiz.explain: A smart chatbot for a specific purpose is built through a system prompt — the instruction that sets its identity, rules, and behaviour.

quiz.q: You should test your prototype with at least one person outside your own team before the day ends.
quiz.explain: True. Testing with an outside person is one of the day's goals — their confusion and reactions are your most valuable data for tomorrow.

rows: Stand-up: each team shares their Day 8 goal in 60 seconds
Break + peer check-in (show your progress to another team)
Build Sprint 2 — refine + test with someone outside your team
End-of-day demo: show what exists, no matter how rough
Reflection — what works, what's broken, tomorrow's plan
